_format_scope: composition + arrangement + performance + stylistic_figures. Excludes mixing/mastering/recording — user handles those via Mix Analyzer on the produced track.
_high_confidence_fields: structural_blueprint (piano+voice opening then drums and strings layer in)
instrumentation (piano, vocal, ondes Martenot, drums, strings)
stylistic_figures around the asymmetric piano figure and the late drum entry
compositional ambiguity between rubato feel and strict meter
_to_verify_fields: tempo_bpm exact citation (75-78 range commonly cited)
exact chord voicings of the piano cycle (notation varies by transcriber)
specific drum pattern reading (whether the kit performance lines up with the 3+3+4+3+3 grouping or floats independently)
key area F# minor vs alternate readings
time_signature: 16/8 most commonly analyzed, with internal grouping 3+3+4+3+3 (sums to 16 eighth-notes per measure). Frequently described as 'feels like rubato but is strictly metered'. The asymmetric internal grouping is the signature compositional gesture — the listener never gets a square downbeat pulse despite the music being fully notated and reproducible.
key_area: F# minor area, with significant chromatic motion across the piano cycle. The chord progression descends through several voicings that include extensions and chromatic neighbor tones — some transcribers read it as a straight i–V–IV–i pattern, others as a more chromatic descent through F#m, A, G#7, Em, etc. The harmonic motion sounds melancholy-modal but is harder to functionally analyze than typical rock progressions.
tuning_documented: N/A — keyboard-led composition. The piano is the principal harmonic instrument; no guitar.

composition.structural_blueprint: piano_alone_intro (the descending piano figure cycles 2-3 times unaccompanied, establishing the asymmetric meter and the harmonic loop) -> vocal_entry_over_piano (Yorke's vocal joins, floating over the piano cycle without locking strictly to its accents) -> ondes_martenot_layer_emerges (the eerie sustained electronic-strings tone layers in, adding harmonic atmosphere) -> drums_enter_late (the kit enters approximately halfway through the track in an unusual half-time-feel that does not square the meter — drums float against the piano grouping rather than locking it) -> string_arrangement_thickens (strings/orchestral textures fill out the harmonic field at the climax) -> fade_outro (instrumentation thins, piano cycle persists into fade)

section_count_and_lengths.piano_intro: Approximately 2-3 cycles of the 16/8 figure unaccompanied
section_count_and_lengths.vocal_section: Multiple iterations of the cycle with vocal phrases of varying length floating over
section_count_and_lengths.drum_entry_point: Approximately halfway into the track — a structural pivot that adds rhythmic ambiguity rather than clarity
section_count_and_lengths.climax_with_strings: The section where strings, ondes Martenot, drums, vocal, and piano are all simultaneously active — densest moment
section_count_and_lengths.outro_fade: Long fade with piano cycle persisting, layers dropping out
section_count_and_lengths._note: The track does not segment into traditional verse/chorus. It is a single evolving meditation over a repeating asymmetric harmonic loop, with structural events being the entries of new instrumental layers.

composition.harmonic_motion: Repeating chromatic descent in the piano figure. The progression cycles every 16/8 measure with distinct voicings rather than identical repetitions — each cycle has subtle micro-variation in the upper voice. The motion is predominantly stepwise downward in the bass, suggesting a Phrygian-tinged minor-key descent. Not a classical functional progression — more of a harmonic loop that creates color through voice-leading rather than through cadential resolution.
composition.modal_choice: F# Aeolian/Phrygian area. The chromatic descent and the absence of strong dominant-tonic resolution put it in modal territory rather than functional minor. The mood is melancholy-modal, supported by the slow tempo and the asymmetric meter.
composition.melodic_contour: Yorke's vocal is predominantly stepwise and falling, with occasional rising leaps for emphasis. The phrasing is rubato-feeling — Yorke does not strictly align his vocal phrases to the piano's metric grouping, which creates the floating, drifting quality. Phrases hang above the piano accompaniment rather than landing on it.
composition.characteristic_riff_construction: The piano cycle is the song's identity — a descending chordal figure in 16/8 played close-voiced in the middle register of the piano. The figure exploits the asymmetric grouping (3+3+4+3+3 eighth-notes) to create a sense of constantly displaced downbeat. The bass note descent provides forward motion; the upper voicing creates harmonic color. This is one of the most distinctive piano figures in 21st-century rock — instantly recognizable from the first few measures.
composition.harmonic_pacing: Slow at the chord-change level (one full cycle per measure of 16/8 = approximately one harmonic 'event' every 12-15 seconds at the song's tempo). Pacing is internal to each measure (chord changes within the cycle) rather than between measures.
composition.phrase_symmetry: Asymmetric BY DESIGN at the meter level (16/8 with 3+3+4+3+3 internal grouping is not square), but symmetric at the cycle level (every measure is the same 16-beat unit). The asymmetry is internal-rhythmic rather than between-phrases. Yorke's vocal phrases are themselves variable in length, often spanning multiple cycles asymmetrically — the vocal floats above the regular metric grid.

instrumentation_complete: Piano (acoustic, principal harmonic and rhythmic instrument)
Ondes Martenot (the eerie sustained electronic-string tone — Jonny Greenwood's signature instrument, used as atmospheric layer)
Drum kit (entering approximately halfway through, played in an unusual feel that floats against the piano meter)
String arrangement / orchestral texture (filling the climax section)
Bass (sparse, possibly upright bass or subtle synth bass)

section_instrumentation.piano_intro: Piano alone, possibly with very faint sub-bass underpinning
section_instrumentation.ondes_layer_emergence: Piano + lead vocal + ondes Martenot atmospheric pad
section_instrumentation.drum_entry: All previous layers + drum kit entering in floating feel
section_instrumentation.climax_with_strings: Piano + vocal + ondes + drums + strings — densest texture
section_instrumentation.outro: Layers drop progressively, piano cycle persists into fade

arrangement.vocal_layering_strategy: Single-tracked lead vocal, slightly atmospheric reverb but not heavily processed in the manner of Everything In Its Right Place. The vocal is foregrounded as a distinct narrative element floating over the instrumental texture. No vocal looping or sample-manipulation — this is a more traditional vocal arrangement than Kid A's experimental approach.
arrangement.dynamic_arc_overall: Slow, monotonically-rising swell. Spare opening (piano alone) -> add vocal -> add ondes -> add drums -> add strings -> peak at full ensemble texture -> fade. The dynamic range is moderate; perceived intensity rises with textural accumulation more than with loudness. Drumless first half versus drum-bearing second half is the principal dynamic event.

instrumental_role_assignment.guitar_role: N/A — no guitar in this track
instrumental_role_assignment.bass_role: Sparse foundational anchor — present at phrase boundaries marking the F# tonic, but not driving
instrumental_role_assignment.drums_role: Late-entering rhythmic-textural layer. Plays in a feel that does NOT lock the piano meter — floats against it, creating polyrhythmic ambiguity. Phil Selway's drumming here is a study in restraint and feel-against-feel.
instrumental_role_assignment.vocal_role: Lead narrative carrier. The vocal performance is rubato-feeling and floats above the strict piano meter — Yorke's phrasing is the song's principal rhythmic ambiguity.

harmonic_density_per_section.piano_intro: Low — single piano cycle, no upper-voice elaboration
harmonic_density_per_section.vocal_entry: Low — vocal adds melody line, no chord stack
harmonic_density_per_section.ondes_layer: Medium — sustained ondes adds harmonic atmosphere underneath the piano
harmonic_density_per_section.drum_entry: Medium — rhythmic activity adds without thickening harmony
harmonic_density_per_section.climax_strings: High — full orchestral texture stacks pseudo-chord harmonics

arrangement_anti_patterns_avoided: No traditional verse-chorus structure (the song is one continuous textural evolution)
No drum kit from the start (the late drum entry is itself a structural event)
No square 4/4 alternative reading — the band commits fully to the asymmetric meter
No obvious instrumental solo (the climax is textural orchestral fullness, not a featured solo)

performance.tempo_feel_description: Slow, rubato-feeling but actually strictly metered. The piano performance is precisely on the grid yet feels free because the asymmetric grouping displaces expected downbeats. Yorke's vocal is the ONLY truly rubato-feeling element — he phrases his lines without strictly aligning to the piano's metric accents, which creates the song's signature floating quality. The drums, when they enter, do NOT square the meter into 4/4 — they float as well, creating a polyrhythmic floating-feel against the piano.

drum_style.feel: Restrained, sparse, feel-floating-against-piano. Selway is widely credited as one of the great restraint drummers in alternative rock, and Pyramid Song is a canonical example. He plays in a feel that does not lock the asymmetric piano meter — instead his kit floats freely, creating polyrhythmic interest against the piano grid.
techniques_documented: Late entry (approximately halfway through the track) — drums are themselves a structural event
Restrained dynamics — Selway plays softly, with brushes or low-volume sticks, supporting the texture rather than driving it
Feel-against-feel: the kit groove implies a different metric reading than the piano's strict 16/8 grouping, creating a sense of two simultaneous time feels
drum_style.groove_feel_alignment: Deliberately misaligned with the piano's strict grid — the polyrhythmic floating between drum feel and piano meter is part of the song's ambiguous time-perception
drum_style.fills_and_transitions: Minimal fills. Drum entries and exits are the structural transitions; within sections the drums sustain a steady understated pattern.

guitar_style.performer: N/A — no guitar in this track

vocal_delivery.verse_delivery: Hushed, intimate, slightly held-back. Mid-register, minimal vibrato. Phrasing rubato-feeling — Yorke does not strictly align his lines to the piano's metric accents, which gives the song its floating quality. The delivery is restrained and meditative rather than projected.
vocal_delivery.outro_delivery: Vocal trails off as instrumentation thins; no climactic vocal moment — the climax is textural-orchestral, not vocal
documented_vocal_quirks: Rubato-feeling phrasing over a strict-metered piano accompaniment — the principal performance ambiguity of the track
Restrained dynamic — Yorke does not project, sustaining the meditative atmosphere
Yorke's vocal is foregrounded but not centered as a 'soaring' lead — it is a floating narrative over the instrumental texture

performance_anti_patterns_avoided: No squaring the meter into 4/4 (would lose the floating quality)
No vocal melisma or held high notes (would draw attention to performance technique rather than texture)
No drum fills marking transitions (drums float rather than drive)
No metric clarification by the band (the ambiguity is the point)

stylistic_figures._note: Production gestures and figures of style — the rises, drops, transitions, climaxes, special effects that define the track's compositional identity beyond pure pitch/rhythm content. Cross-mining target.

transitions_between_sections.description: Yorke's voice simply enters on a downbeat of the piano cycle — no fill, no riser, no preparation. The piano continues unchanged.

The drums enter approximately halfway through the track. There is no riser, no fill, no anticipation — Selway simply begins playing, and his floating feel against the piano meter immediately establishes a polyrhythmic ambiguity. This is one of the most studied late-drum-entries in alternative rock.
The orchestral textures and drums drop out one by one over the outro, leaving the piano cycle alone to fade. Inverse of the cumulative build of the first half.

risers_and_builds.duration_bars: the entire track functions as one slow build
risers_and_builds.description: The build here is not a riser in the synth sense — it is the slow accumulation of acoustic and orchestral layers (piano alone -> +vocal -> +ondes Martenot -> +drums -> +strings) over approximately 4 minutes. The energy rises through orchestration density rather than through synthesized sweeps.

cutoffs_and_filter_moves.description: Pyramid Song is an acoustic-orchestral arrangement and does not use synthesizer filter automation. The 'tonal evolution' is achieved through orchestration changes (which instruments are active), not through filter sweeps.

drops_and_breakdowns.description: The track has no drops in the dance sense. The arrangement only ADDS layers across the track, then SUBTRACTS them in the outro — there is no mid-track drop that resets and rebuilds. The structure is monotonically cumulative then a single descent.

climax_moments.where: approximately the last third of the track
climax_moments.description: Maximum overlap of piano + vocal + ondes Martenot + drums + strings. The harmonic and timbral content is at its richest. Not a loud climax — moderate dynamic — but maximally ORCHESTRAL.

special_effects_and_textural_devices.description: Jonny Greenwood plays the ondes Martenot, an early electronic instrument with a unique sustained-string-like timbre. Its eerie quality adds an otherworldly atmosphere that distinguishes the track from a purely acoustic arrangement. The ondes is used as a slow-moving harmonic pad rather than as a melodic lead.

Yorke's vocal phrasing is rubato-feeling (floating, not metrically aligned), while the piano accompaniment is strictly on the 3+3+4+3+3 grid. The tension between the two creates the song's signature floating quality.

When Selway's drums enter, they imply a different metric reading than the piano's strict 16/8 grouping. The two grids float against each other rather than locking — a polyrhythmic strategy that maintains the song's metric ambiguity even after drums arrive.

the_moment_people_remember.primary: The opening piano cycle in 16/8 — instantly recognizable from the first measures, simultaneously feeling rubato and being strictly metered. One of the most distinctive piano figures in 21st-century rock.
the_moment_people_remember.secondary: The late drum entry approximately halfway through — Selway's floating feel against the piano grid is a canonical example of restraint drumming and metric ambiguity
the_moment_people_remember.tertiary: The orchestral climax with strings and ondes Martenot fully engaged — the moment when the texture reaches its richest density
